title: Use three-bucket charging sequence on 80-tonne EAF, never four buckets
rationale: Four-bucket charges create too much dead time and lose hot heel benefit

title: First bucket composition: 60% shred, 30% #1 heavy melt, 10% low-residual prime at bottom
Place low-residual prime bridge layer at bottom of first bucket over hot heel
rationale: Prevents electrodes from punching through into heel and causing arc flash

title: Second bucket: 80% shred with pig iron layer on top
rationale: Pig iron layer feeds bath chemistry

title: Never put turnings or borings in first bucket
rationale: Turnings and borings pack down and choke hot heel mixing

title: Limit borings to maximum 5% of total weight in third bucket only
Run bore-in at 70-80% secondary voltage on tap 7, low impedance for short arc
scope: 80-tonne EAF power profile during bore-in stage

title: Switch to tap 12-13 at full secondary (~950V) after flat bath achieved
Flat bath achieved at 25 min cold start or 18 min hot heel start
Increase power 5-10% for every 100mm of foamy slag above arc tip
scope: 80-tonne EAF power optimization with slag coverage

title: Throttle back power if foamy slag not covering arc
scope: 80-tonne EAF power control for roof protection

title: Maintain electrode regulator response time under 200 milliseconds
Grid flicker complaints indicate arc too short, check regulator response
Target basicity 1.8-2.2 for low-carbon plain rebar grades
rationale: Proper basicity range for effective refining of low-carbon steel

title: Target basicity 2.4-2.6 for higher-carbon and HSLA grades
rationale: Higher basicity needed for better phosphorus and sulphur partitioning

title: Target FeO content 18-25% during EAF refining
rationale: Optimal oxidation potential range for phosphorus oxidation without yield loss

title: FeO below 15% prevents phosphorus oxidation and causes reversion
rationale: Insufficient oxidation potential at low FeO levels

title: FeO above 30% causes iron loss and yield drop
rationale: Excessive oxidation leads to iron units lost to slag

title: Sample slag at slag door every 10 minutes during refining for FeO measurement
rationale: Regular monitoring needed to control oxidation potential

title: Add lime in 200-300 kg charges, never dump full tonne at once
rationale: Large lime additions form unmelted clumps that take 15 minutes to dissolve

title: Always inject carbon under slag, not on surface
rationale: Surface injection burns off uselessly without benefit

title: Maintain 400-600 mm foamy slag coverage over arc during flat bath stage
rationale: Without foam, arc radiates heat into sidewall instead of bath, electrode tip wears 30% faster, and refractory life drops by half

title: Inject carbon at 1.5-2 kg/tonne at 8-12 bar pressure for slag foaming
Position carbon injection lance 30-45 degrees below horizontal pointed at slag-metal interface
Check FeO level, slag basicity, and carbon delivery rate in order when foaming fails
Carbon reduces FeO to iron and CO bubbles which foams the slag
Tap carbon injection lance with steel rod every 4 hours to check for clogs
Tap temperature range 1610-1640°C for low-carbon rebar based on tundish distance and treatments
Measure tap temperature with disposable thermocouple before tap, never trust bath reading alone
Bath meniscus temperature 15-25°C colder than bulk metal temperature
Tap at maximum 80% flow rate through EBT, never full open to prevent slag entrainment
Use argon shroud 60-100 Nm3/hr through refractory tube around tap stream
Tapping without argon shroud increases nitrogen from 40 ppm to over 100 ppm causing age cracking
Target tap weight 75-78 tonnes maintaining 2-4 tonnes hot heel for next heat
Below 1.5 tonnes heel causes rough bore-in, above 5 tonnes loses capacity
Check heel composition every shift by lance sampling for carbon drift monitoring
rationale: Monitors slag performance and prevents carbon reversion

title: Carbon drift over 0.05% between heats indicates slag not performing and carbon reverting from heel
rationale: Process diagnostic indicator

title: Maintain heel temperature 1500-1530°C at start of next charge
rationale: Prevents scrap freezing below 1500°C and energy waste above 1530°C

title: Apply 50mm basic powder slag cover on heel during turnaround
rationale: Slows heat loss and prevents oxidation

title: Add 50-80 kg carbon to heel if not charging within 15 minutes of tap
rationale: Prevents heel surface oxidation and energy losses

title: Run secondary oxygen at 8-12 Nm3/min during CO evolution for post-combustion
rationale: Burns CO to CO2 releasing 280 MJ/kg CO for energy recovery

title: Target freeboard temperature 1600-1700°C during post-combustion
rationale: Indicates optimal CO combustion - colder means missing CO, hotter damages duct refractory

title: Monitor freeboard temperature with thermocouples 2 metres downstream of post-combustion zone
rationale: Cleanest measurement point for post-combustion temperature control

title: Post-combustion system saves 25-30 kWh/tonne (2.4 MWh per 80-tonne heat)
rationale: Energy savings from capturing CO combustion heat instead of losing it

title: EAF bottom refractory is shotcreted MgO-C with carbon binder lasting 8000-12000 heats
rationale: Specific material composition and expected lifetime range for bottom refractory

title: EAF sidewall refractory above slag line lasts 800-1200 heats
rationale: Specific lifetime range for sidewall refractory between water-cooled panels

title: EAF roof refractory life is 200-400 heats depending on naked arc frequency
rationale: Roof refractory lifetime range and primary wear factor

title: EAF roof hot spots are at 4 and 8 o'clock positions in electrode triangle
rationale: Specific locations that receive most arc radiation when foam coverage is inadequate

title: Apply hot fettling patches with 5-10 kg MgO gunite in 30 seconds followed by water spray
rationale: Standard hot patching procedure for roof refractory repair during turnaround

title: More than 3 refractory patches per shift indicates faulty foam practice
rationale: Diagnostic threshold indicating foam coverage problems rather than normal refractory wear

title: Standard graphite electrodes cost $4000-5000 per tonne
rationale: Cost structure data for electrode consumption economics

title: EAF electrode consumption is 1.6-2.0 kg per tonne of liquid steel (130-160 kg per 80-tonne heat)
rationale: Consumption rate benchmark for electrode usage

title: Electrode consumption breakdown: 60% sidewall oxidation, 30% tip wear from arc, 10% stub-end loss
rationale: Root cause analysis for electrode consumption management

title: Inject argon through electrode cooling collar at 5-8 Nm3/hr per electrode to reduce sidewall oxidation
rationale: Specific procedure to reduce 60% of electrode consumption from sidewall oxidation

title: Change electrodes only when remaining length below 800mm to minimize stub loss
rationale: Procedure to minimize 10% of electrode consumption from premature changes

title: Monitor electrode regulator for movements exceeding 6 times per minute indicating excessive regulator gain
rationale: Diagnostic indicator for electrode wear rate optimization

title: Slag indicators are leading, all other EAF measurements are lagging indicators
rationale: Operator emphasized slag as real-time feedback versus temperature, current, voltage, and power being lagging indicators

title: Senior melters can visually determine FeO content within 2% accuracy by slag color
rationale: Operator stated experienced melters achieve 2% accuracy on FeO estimation through visual slag assessment

title: Prioritize refractory life over short-term productivity gains on EAF
rationale: Operator explained 90 seconds saved per heat by pushing power on thin foam costs 50 heats of roof life, making the economics unfavorable

title: EAF roof patch costs $8000, full roof rebuild costs $200000
rationale: Operator provided specific cost figures for roof refractory maintenance and replacement
